Details:
A full-scale study showed ultraviolet irradiation to be ineffective for removing most of the PFASs sampled, including perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS), perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA), perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA), and perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA). However, the study observed partial removal (an average of 38 percent) of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS). Partial removals (32 to 56 percent) also were observed in a few samples for perfluorohexylsulfonate (PFHxS) and fluorotelomer sulfonate 6:2 (FtS 6:2) [2441].

Bench-scale studies [1697, 1698, 1699, 1701, 1702, 1708, 2607, 2614, 2640, 2644] have shown that degradation of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) using ultraviolet (UV) irradiation varies greatly, depending on UV wavelength, UV contact time, and the addition of chemical catalysts. Tests that evaluated perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) degradation with UV that included 185 nm UV light achieved removal percentages that increased from 57 to 99 percent removal as contact time increased from one hour to four hours [1697, 1698, 1699, 2614]. Tests using 254 nm UV light alone achieved 4 to 34 percent removal of PFOA [1697, 1698, 1699, 1702, 1708, 2614]. One additional study achieved 44 to 89.5 percent PFOA removal using wavelengths in the 220 to 460 nm range in combination with very long contact times of 24 to 72 hours [1701].

Bench-scale studies [2607, 2644] found that UV degradation of various PFAS compounds can be enhanced (achieving up to 99 percent removal, depending on the specific PFAS) with the addition of sulfite using a contact time of 30 minutes or greater. One of these studies [2644] suggested this approach would be promising at scale in groundwater remediation but did not evaluate whether it would be practical for use in drinking water applications.

Additional bench-scale studies found that UV degradation of PFOA can be enhanced (achieving up to greater than 99 percent removal) with the addition of other chemicals/catalysts including sodium periodate [1697], persulfate [1698], iron [1708], and the combination of gallium oxide and peroxymonosulfate [2640]. All of these tests involved contact times of 30 minutes or greater. 
One study using a photocatalytic reactor membrane pilot system, employing UV/TiO2 photocatalysis, found 15 percent photodegradation of PFOS for surface water spiked with low levels of 32 pharmaceuticals and EDCs (ng/L to ug/L range) [2172]. Another pilot-scale study utilizing UV/TiO2 and UV with chlorine photocatalysis saw an increase in concentrations of all PFAS compounds studied in all scenarios [2847].
